section.title: Against False Prophets
section.sectionRationale: Jerusalem's true danger was never only the Babylonian army; it was the prophets and prophetesses who papered over that danger with comforting lies. Ezekiel exposes both the male prophets who whitewash a crumbling wall with false peace and the women who traffic in magic charms, warning that leaders who tell people what they want to hear will fall with the very structures they falsely reinforced.
section.thesis: God condemns prophets who speak from their own imagination rather than His true word, promising peace where there is no peace and propping up a doomed city with whitewash instead of truth, showing that false comfort is more dangerous than open warning, and that only the true Word of God, ultimately Christ Himself, can be trusted to build on solid ground.
section.frameNote: Three movements: woe pronounced on prophets who follow their own spirit and have seen nothing true; the image of a flimsy wall whitewashed with false peace, doomed to fall in the coming storm; and woe pronounced on prophetesses who ensnare souls with magic charms for a handful of barley.

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, prophesy against the prophets of Israel who are now prophesying. Tell those who prophesy out of their own imagination: Hear the word of the LORD!
This is what the Lord GOD says: Woe to the foolish prophets who follow their own spirit, yet have seen nothing.
Your prophets, O Israel, are like foxes among the ruins.
You did not go up to the gaps or restore the wall around the house of Israel so that it would stand in the battle on the Day of the LORD.
They see {{chazon_shav|false visions}} and speak lying divinations. They claim, ‘Thus declares the LORD,’ when the LORD did not send them; yet they wait for the fulfillment of their message.
Haven’t you seen a false vision and spoken a lying divination when you proclaim, ‘Thus declares the LORD,’ even though I have not spoken?
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: Because you have uttered vain words and seen false visions, I am against you, declares the Lord GOD.

My hand will be against the prophets who see false visions and speak lying divinations. They will not belong to the council of My people or be recorded in the register of the house of Israel, nor will they enter the land of Israel. Then you will know that I am the Lord GOD.

units.label: The whitewashed wall
units.note: false peace over a crumbling foundation

verses.text: Because they have led My people astray, saying, ‘Peace,’ when there is no peace, and whitewashing any flimsy wall that is built,
tell those {{tapel|whitewashing}} the wall that it will fall. Rain will come in torrents, I will send hailstones plunging down, and a windstorm will burst forth.
Surely when the wall has fallen, you will not be asked, ‘Where is the whitewash with which you covered it?’
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: In My wrath I will release a windstorm, and in My anger torrents of rain and hail will fall with destructive fury.

I will tear down the wall you whitewashed and level it to the ground, so that its foundation is exposed. The city will fall, and you will be destroyed within it. Then you will know that I am the LORD.
And after I have vented My wrath against the wall and against those who whitewashed it, I will say to you: ‘The wall is gone, and so are those who whitewashed it,
those prophets of Israel who prophesied to Jerusalem and saw a vision of peace for her when there was no peace, declares the Lord GOD.’
Now, O son of man, set your face against the daughters of your people who prophesy out of their own imagination. Prophesy against them

and tell them that this is what the Lord GOD says: Woe to the women who sew {{kesatot|magic charms}} on their wrists and make veils for the heads of people of every height, in order to ensnare their souls. Will you ensnare the souls of My people but preserve your own?

You have profaned Me among My people for handfuls of barley and scraps of bread. By lying to My people who would listen, you have killed those who should not have died and spared those who should not have lived.

Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: See, I am against the magic charms with which you ensnare souls like birds, and I will tear them from your arms. So I will free the souls you have ensnared like birds.
I will also tear off your veils and deliver My people from your hands, so that they will no longer be prey in your hands. Then you will know that I am the LORD.
Because you have disheartened the righteous with your lies, even though I have caused them no grief, and because you have encouraged the wicked not to turn from their evil ways to save their lives,
therefore you will no longer see false visions or practice divination. I will deliver My people from your hands. Then you will know that I am the LORD.”

chazon_shav.gloss: False, empty, or worthless vision. These prophets claimed genuine revelation, 'Thus declares the LORD,' while seeing nothing real, manufacturing comforting messages from their own imagination rather than receiving them from God.

tapel.gloss: Whitewash, or untempered plaster smeared over a weak wall to hide its cracks. The image pictures false prophets covering Jerusalem's real, structural danger with a thin, cosmetic layer of reassurance that cannot survive the coming storm.

kesatot.gloss: Magic bands or charms sewn onto the wrists, used in occult practices claiming to control or manipulate life and death. God tears these very charms from the prophetesses' arms, showing His power to free the souls their sorcery had ensnared.

basic.context: Jerusalem faced a danger even greater than the coming Babylonian siege: prophets and prophetesses who told the people exactly what they wanted to hear. Ezekiel exposes both groups, the men who whitewashed a crumbling wall with false assurances of peace, and the women who used magic charms to manipulate and profit from vulnerable souls.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a direct, historical indictment of specific religious leaders operating among Ezekiel's exiled community and in Jerusalem, not a general parable. The plain, uncomfortable truth is that comforting words are not automatically true words, and that God holds those who claim to speak for Him to the strictest account when they have not.
basic.originalLanguages: These prophets peddle a chazon shav, an empty or worthless vision, dressed up in the language of true revelation. Their message is tapel, whitewash, a thin cosmetic covering over real structural danger. The prophetesses use kesatot, magic charms, literally binding people's wrists with occult manipulation for a handful of barley.
basic.typology: The whitewashed wall that looks solid but cannot withstand the storm anticipates Jesus' parable of the wise and foolish builders, Matthew 7:24-27, where only a house built on the rock of His words stands when the storm comes; false comfort, however sincere, cannot substitute for a true foundation.
basic.christ: Jesus repeatedly warns of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing, Matthew 7:15, and who will arise claiming great signs, Matthew 24:24, standing in direct continuity with Ezekiel's warning; and where these prophets falsely cried 'Peace, peace' when there was no peace, Christ alone is the one who can truly say 'Peace I leave with you', John 14:27, because His peace is secured by an atonement that actually deals with sin rather than papering over it.
basic.god: God is jealous for the truth of His own name; He will not let those who falsely claim to speak for Him escape accountability, and He is personally committed to freeing the souls ensnared by manipulative religion, tearing the very charms from the deceivers' arms.
basic.crossReferences: Jeremiah 6:14 records the same charge, 'They have healed the brokenness of My people superficially, saying, Peace, peace, but there is no peace.' Matthew 7:15-23 and 24:24 warn of false prophets and false christs. Matthew 7:24-27 contrasts building on rock versus sand. 1 John 4:1 commands testing every spirit.
basic.oneStory: This is the exposure of religious deception at its most dangerous, comforting words mistaken for God's true word. Jesus stands in continuity with this warning, cautioning His own followers against false prophets, and offers Himself as the only sure foundation and the only true peace, one that can actually withstand the storm these whitewashed walls could not.

context.literary: The chapter is structured in parallel halves, first addressing male prophets with the image of a whitewashed wall, then addressing female prophetesses with the image of ensnaring charms, both units following the same pattern of indictment, judgment, and vindication of God's people.
context.historical: Delivered among the exiles in Babylon, this passage confronts prophets both in Jerusalem and possibly among the exiled community itself who were assuring people that exile would be brief and Jerusalem would not fall, false comfort that events soon proved catastrophically wrong.
context.canonical: Echoes Jeremiah's own contemporary struggle against false prophets crying 'Peace, peace' when there was no peace, Jeremiah 6:14, 8:11, and anticipates the New Testament's repeated warnings against false teachers and false prophets who trade truth for popularity or profit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose and condemn religious leaders, both men and women, who manufactured comforting prophecies from their own imagination rather than from God, and to assure the genuinely faithful that God Himself would judge these deceivers and rescue His people from their manipulation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes and condemns a specific historical abuse of prophetic office; it is not prescribing a manual for identifying false prophets today, though its underlying principles, testing claims against God's actual word and watching for manipulation or profit-seeking, remain permanently relevant.

debates.question: Were the women in verses 17-23 practicing formal prophecy alongside recognized male prophets, or a separate, folk-level occult practice?

views.label: A parallel, if illegitimate, prophetic office
views.case: The text calls them 'daughters who prophesy', using the same prophetic vocabulary as the men in verses 1-16, suggesting they claimed a comparable, if false, prophetic authority within the community.

views.label: Folk magic distinct from formal prophecy
views.case: The specific charge, sewing charms and veils for payment, sounds more like private occult practice, divination for hire, than public prophetic ministry, suggesting a related but somewhat different abuse being addressed in parallel.

debates.bearing: Either way, both groups are condemned for the same root sin, manufacturing false spiritual authority to manipulate and profit from vulnerable people rather than speaking God's actual word.

study.originalLanguages: These prophets peddle chazon shav, an empty or worthless vision, dressed in the language of genuine revelation, 'Thus declares the LORD.' Their comforting message is tapel, whitewash, a thin cosmetic covering hiding real structural danger beneath. The prophetesses bind people with kesatot, magic charms, for a handful of barley, and God personally tears these charms from their arms to free the souls they had ensnared.

typology.type: The whitewashed wall that collapses in the storm, Ezekiel 13:10-15
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus' parable of the wise and foolish builders, Matthew 7:24-27, where only a house founded on His words withstands the storm.

typology.type: Prophets crying 'Peace' when there is no peace, Ezekiel 13:10,16
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with Christ, the only one who can truly grant lasting peace, John 14:27, because His peace rests on an atonement that actually resolves sin.

study.christ: Jesus repeatedly warns His own followers of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing, Matthew 7:15, and of false christs and false prophets who will arise performing great signs, Matthew 24:24, standing in direct continuity with Ezekiel's warning here. Where these prophets falsely cried 'Peace, peace' over a doomed city, Christ alone can truly say, 'Peace I leave with you; My peace I give you,' John 14:27, because His peace is secured by a real atonement, not a cosmetic cover over unresolved sin.
study.god: God is jealous for the truth of His own name and will not allow those who falsely claim to speak for Him to escape accountability; at the same time, He is personally and tenderly committed to freeing the souls ensnared by manipulative religion, tearing the very charms from the deceivers' arms to rescue His people.

crossReferences.note: Records the same charge against false prophets who cried 'Peace, peace' when there was no peace.
Jesus warns of false prophets known by their fruit, coming in sheep's clothing.
Contrasts building on rock versus sand, echoing the whitewashed wall that cannot withstand the storm.
Commands believers to test every spirit rather than believing every claim to speak for God.

study.oneStory: This is the exposure of religious deception at its most dangerous, comforting words mistaken for God's true word, propping up a doomed structure with a thin coat of false peace. Jesus stands in direct continuity with this warning, cautioning His own followers against false prophets, and offers Himself as the only sure foundation and the only true peace, one that actually withstands the storm these whitewashed walls could not.

sermon.title: Genuine or False Prophets? (Ezek. 13-14)
sermon.why: Carson works directly through Ezekiel 13, contrasting false prophets who manufacture comforting visions from their own imagination with the marks of genuine, Spirit-given prophecy, drawing out principles for discernment that this study applies to the whitewashed wall and the magic charms alike.

quiz.q: What is the fundamental charge God brings against the prophets in verses 1-9?

options.text: They prophesy out of their own imagination, claiming 'Thus declares the LORD' when He never spoke to them.
options.note: Verses 2-3 and 6-7 make this charge explicit and repeated.

options.text: They refused to speak to the people at all.
options.note: The problem is that they spoke constantly, but falsely, not that they were silent.

options.text: They demanded too much payment for their services.
options.note: Payment is addressed later regarding the prophetesses, verse 19, but this initial charge concerns false, self-generated visions.

options.text: They correctly predicted judgment but were ignored.
options.note: The problem is the opposite: they predicted false peace and comfort, not accurate judgment.

quiz.q: What does the image of the whitewashed wall represent?

options.text: False prophets covering Jerusalem's real, structural danger with a thin layer of comforting reassurance that cannot survive God's coming judgment.
options.note: Verses 10-15 develop this image as a direct picture of false, superficial peace.

options.text: A genuine, well-built defense that successfully protected the city.
options.note: The wall is explicitly flimsy and doomed to fall, not a genuine defense.

options.text: The temple's literal architecture needing repair.
options.note: The wall is a metaphor for false assurance, not a literal architectural report.

options.text: A minor cosmetic issue with no real consequence.
options.note: The consequence is severe: the wall's collapse pictures the city's coming destruction.

quiz.q: What specific practice are the prophetesses in verses 17-23 condemned for?

options.text: Sewing magic charms on wrists and making veils to ensnare souls for profit, handfuls of barley and scraps of bread.
options.note: Verses 18-19 describe this exact practice and its motive of profit.

options.text: Refusing to prophesy at all out of fear.
options.note: They actively prophesied and practiced occult manipulation, rather than remaining silent.

options.text: Teaching the law faithfully but too strictly.
options.note: Their offense is manipulative sorcery for profit, not strict but faithful teaching.

options.text: Publicly opposing the male false prophets.
options.note: The text presents them as engaging in a parallel abuse, not as opponents of the male prophets.

quiz.q: What does God promise to do about the charms these women use?

options.text: Tear them from their arms and free the souls ensnared by them.
options.note: Verse 20 states God will personally tear away the charms and liberate the ensnared souls.

options.text: Bless the charms so they work for good instead.
options.note: God opposes and destroys the charms entirely; He does not repurpose them.

options.text: Ignore the charms since they have no real spiritual power.
options.note: God takes direct, personal action against them rather than ignoring the practice.

options.text: Allow the women to keep using them if they repent.
options.note: God's stated response is to tear the charms away and end the practice entirely, not to negotiate continued use.

quiz.q: What is the ultimate outcome God promises for these false prophets and prophetesses?

options.text: They will be excluded from the council of God's people, and their false visions and divinations will end.
options.note: Verses 9 and 23 both state this outcome, that their prophetic deception will be brought to an end and they will lose their standing among God's people.

options.text: They will be promoted to positions of greater national influence.
options.note: The passage promises their downfall and exclusion, the opposite of promotion.

options.text: They will be given a final chance to prophesy accurately.
options.note: The text describes ending their practice and influence, not granting further prophetic opportunity.

options.text: They will be forgiven immediately without consequence.
options.note: The passage describes real, severe consequences: exclusion from the community and the end of their false practices.

questions: The false prophets told people what they wanted to hear rather than what was true. Where in your own life do you prefer comforting words over honest ones, even at the cost of the truth?
The whitewashed wall looked solid but could not survive the storm. What false assurances or shortcuts are you relying on that might not hold up under real pressure or trial?
The prophetesses profited from ensnaring vulnerable souls with manipulative promises. Where might you be tempted to use spiritual language to control or manipulate others, even with good intentions?

God personally promises to tear the charms from the deceivers' arms and free the ensnared souls. Where do you need to trust that God actively fights for those trapped by false teaching or manipulation, rather than leaving them to escape alone?
Jesus warns that false prophets will come in sheep's clothing. What practical habits help you test what you hear, in preaching, online, or from trusted voices, against the actual word of God?

translationNotes.note: The comparison of the prophets to 'foxes among the ruins' pictures scavengers exploiting a broken-down city for their own advantage rather than repairing it, an image of self-interested leadership abandoning real responsibility.

Some translations preserve a dash or extended clause across verses 15 and 16 in the original punctuation; this text renders it as a continuous sentence identifying 'those who whitewashed it' directly with 'those prophets of Israel' in the following verse.

The exact nature of the 'magic charms' and 'veils' is debated among scholars, but the underlying Hebrew terms consistently point to items used in occult practices claiming power over life and death, exploited here for material profit.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit; two instances of em dash punctuation in the source text, verse 15, were rendered with a comma per house style. Sermon link verified via live web search and fetch this session, The Gospel Coalition's Carson Center podcast.
